label: Perplexity
definition: An intrinsic measure of how well a language model predicts a text, defined as the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy; lower means the model is less surprised and assigns the data higher probability. It measures the pretraining objective directly and cheaply, but low perplexity does not guarantee usefulness: it rewards calibrated next-token probability, not correctness, helpfulness, or truth.

body.text: Perplexity is the standard intrinsic metric for a language model: a single number that says how surprised the model is, on average, by the text it is asked to predict. If a model assigns high probability to the words that actually appear, its perplexity is low; if the real text looks unlikely under the model, its perplexity is high. Loosely, a perplexity of N means the model is as uncertain at each step as if it were choosing uniformly among N equally likely options. A perfect model that always assigned probability one to the true next token would have a perplexity of one.

The metric matters because it measures the modeling objective directly, without any downstream task in the loop. Pretraining of a language model minimizes cross-entropy on the corpus, and perplexity is just a monotonic transform of that same quantity, so it tracks training progress faithfully and cheaply. For decades it was the headline number for language modeling, and it remains the fastest way to compare two models on the same held-out data, to detect a regression, or to confirm that a model has actually learned the distribution of a domain rather than memorized it.

Computing perplexity is straightforward. Run the model over a held-out sequence, read off the probability it assigns to each true token given the preceding context, and average the negative log of those probabilities to get the mean cross-entropy. Perplexity is the exponential of that average, base-2 if the log is in bits and base-e if it is in nats. An equivalent and revealing form is the geometric mean of the reciprocal per-token probabilities, which makes clear that a single token the model deemed nearly impossible can dominate the score. A closely related quantity, bits-per-character, simply normalizes by characters instead of tokens so that models with different tokenizers can be compared.

Its central limitation is that it is tokenizer-dependent and corpus-dependent, so perplexity numbers are only comparable when the vocabulary, the tokenization, and the evaluation text are identical. A model that splits text into smaller pieces will tend to report a lower per-token perplexity for trivial reasons, which is why bits-per-character or bits-per-byte is preferred for cross-model comparison. More fundamentally, and this is the keeper, low perplexity does not guarantee usefulness: a model can predict fluent text well yet still hallucinate facts, fail at reasoning, or ignore instructions. Perplexity rewards calibrated next-token probability, not correctness, helpfulness, or truth, which is why it has been displaced as the primary public benchmark by task suites like mmlu.

Perplexity sits at the root of the evaluation family. It is the exponential of cross-entropy, it presupposes a well-calibrated probability distribution (see calibration), and it measures exactly the log-likelihood objective that pretraining optimizes. The benchmarks that report accuracy on questions, such as mmlu, measure something perplexity cannot: whether the fluent, low-surprise text the model produces is also right.
